original_abstract: Electrochemical synthesis offers a sustainable pathway for producing high-value organonitrogen chemicals using renewable energy and environmental pollutants. Here, we investigate the use of BIF-29(Cu), a molecular boron-imidazolate cage featuring isolated copper sites, as an electrocatalyst for the synthesis of urea through the co-reduction of $CO_2$ and $NO_3^-$. Despite its single-atom catalytic centers, BIF-29(Cu) achieves an exceptional urea Faradaic efficiency of 68.5% and a partial current density of 424 µA cm⁻², placing it among the most selective systems reported to date for this transformation. The reaction mechanism was elucidated using a combination of in situ surface-enhanced infrared absorption spectroscopy (SEIRAS), in situ X-ray absorption spectroscopy (XAS), and density functional theory (DFT) calculations. Our findings reveal that the C-N bond formation proceeds via a cascade mechanism: $NO_3^-$ and $CO_2$ are independently reduced at distinct copper sites to generate hydroxylamine ($NH_2OH$) and adsorbed carbon monoxide (*CO), respectively. The subsequent condensation of these intermediates at a single copper site facilitates the final urea production. This study demonstrates the potential of supramolecular metal-organic cages with well-defined, discrete active sites to mediate complex, multi-step coupling reactions with high efficiency and structural stability.
humanized_abstract: Electrochemical synthesis is an emerging strategy to prepare large quantity of high-value organonitrogen compounds using renewable energy and recycled waste. Herein, using the molecular boron-imidazolate cage BIF-29(Cu) which possesses isolated copper species as the electrocatalyst, the urea production through co-reduction of $CO_2$ and $NO_3^-$ is realized at the metal cathode. Surprisingly, though BIF-29(Cu) possesses single-atom catalytic centers, a urea Faradaic efficiency of 68.5% and a partial current density of 424 µA cm⁻² is achieved, outperforming most reported catalysts in urea production. Through combining the in situ surface-enhanced infrared absorption spectroscopy (SEIRAS), in situ X-ray absorption spectroscopy (XAS) and density functional theory (DFT) calculations, the catalytic reaction pathway for the production of urea is revealed. The CO2 reduction and $NO_3^-$ reduction occur at different copper centers, producing hydroxylamine (NH2OH) and *CO through the independent reduction pathway, and the C-N bond is constructed at a single copper site in the follow-up reaction through the catalytic cascade pathway, thereby realizing the production of urea. Therefore, using discrete MOF catalyst with well-defined active sites can not only enhance the structural stability of the catalyst, but also realize efficient synthesis of complex compounds through multi-step catalytic reactions.
